perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means all trade secrets and information disclosed by GK to SUPPLIER, including, but not limited to, type design data, drawings, photographs, specifications, models, prototypes, designs, materials, construction or assembly, computer hardware and softwa re (whether in machine-re adable or human-readable form), technical, commercial and operational information concerning products, information concerning manufacturing methods and techniques, quality control and test methods, marketing data including target customers, customer lists and market plans, cost and pricing data and product applications.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means all trade secrets and information disclosed by GK to SUPPLIER, including, but not limited to, type design data, drawings, photographs, specifications, models, prototypes, designs, materials, construction or assembly, computer hardware and softwa re (whether in machine-re adable or human-readable form), technical, commercial and operational information concerning products, information concerning manufacturing methods and techniques, quality control and test methods, marketing data including target customers, customer lists and market plans, cost and pricing data and product applications. Information is not considered confidential if it is not explicitly marked as such at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change adds a condition to the definition of 'Confidential Information,' stating that information is not confidential unless explicitly marked as such. This contradicts section 4, which implies that information disclosed in non-written form is confidential if summarized in writing within 30 days, regardless of whether it was explicitly marked as confidential at the original time of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. SUPPLIER shall maintain all Confidential Information received from GK in the same manner that the SUPPLIER maintains its own Confidential Information, provided that the standard of care required shall be at least a reasonable standard. Information disclosed in other that written form shall be considered Confidential Information only to the extent GK summarizes the same in a written form that is transmitted to SUPPLIER within thirty (30) calendar days of the non-written disclosures.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds a new condition to the definition of 'Confidential Information': it must be explicitly marked as such at the time of disclosure. However, Section 4 states that even information disclosed in non-written form can be considered confidential if it is summarized in writing within 30 days, regardless of whether it was explicitly marked as confidential at the original time of disclosure. This creates a direct contradiction because, according to the changed text, unmarked oral disclosures that get written up within 30 days would not be considered confidential, but according to Section 4, they are.

perturbation.original_text: 2. SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein, unless explicitly authorized in writing by a SUPPLIER legal counsel.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an exception to the confidentiality obligation, stating that SUPPLIER is allowed to disclose the confidential information if authorised by the SUPPLIER legal counsel, which contradicts the strict and unconditional obligation stated in section 2. This creates an in-text contradiction because section 2 initially mandates strict confidentiality, but the added exception undermines this requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein, unless explicitly authorized in writing by a SUPPLIER legal counsel.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use it. The changed text introduces an exception where SUPPLIER can disclose the confidential information if authorized by the SUPPLIER legal counsel, which directly contradicts the strict and unconditional obligation stated in the initial clause. Since the changed and contradicted text are identical, the added exception undermines the initial requirement.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Confidential Information does not include the following: a) information which was already in the public domain at the time of disclosure; or b) information which, though originally confidential, subsequently becomes part of the public domain through no fault of SUPPLIER; or c) information which was properly in SUPPLIER’s possession prior to receipt thereof from GK, as reasonably evidenced by written records or other writings, or by actual use by SUPPLIER prior to the disclosure by GK. In each case, the (i) burden of pr oof shall lie with SUPPLIER to demonstrate that one of the above-listed exceptions applies and Sign:_____________ Page 2 (ii) SUPPLIER shall notify GKT in writing of SUPPLIER’s intent to make any disclosure based on one of the above-listed exceptions at least 30 days in advance (including a description of the informati on to be disclosed and the basis for the claimed exception).
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Confidential Information does not include the following: a) information which was already in the public domain at the time of disclosure; or b) information which, though originally confidential, subsequently becomes part of the public domain through no fault of SUPPLIER; or c) information which was properly in SUPPLIER’s possession prior to receipt thereof from GK, as reasonably evidenced by written records or other writings, or by actual use by SUPPLIER prior to the disclosure by GK; or d) information disclosed by GK to multiple suppliers. In each case, the (i) burden of pr oof shall lie with SUPPLIER to demonstrate that one of the above-listed exceptions applies and Sign:_____________ Page 2 (ii) SUPPLIER shall notify GKT in writing of SUPPLIER’s intent to make any disclosure based on one of the above-listed exceptions at least 30 days in advance (including a description of the informati on to be disclosed and the basis for the claimed exception).
perturbation.explanation: The addition of clause 'd) information disclosed by GK to multiple suppliers' to the definition of what is *not* confidential information creates a contradiction with the overall purpose of the NDA. The NDA is meant to protect GK's confidential information, but this clause essentially states that if GK discloses the same information to multiple suppliers, it is no longer considered confidential. This significantly undermines the protection afforded by the NDA, contradicting the intent and scope outlined in section 2, which obligates the supplier to hold *all* Confidential Information in strict confidence.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text adds clause (d) to section 3, stating that information disclosed by GK to multiple suppliers is no longer considered confidential. This directly contradicts section 2, which requires the Supplier to hold *all* Confidential Information in strict confidence. The addition of clause (d) weakens the confidentiality obligation outlined in section 2. This is a structural flaw that causes an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees and who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement, but SUPPLIER shall remain responsible to ensure their compliance.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees and who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement. SUPPLIER will not be held responsible to ensure their compliance, as long as they have advised the employee of their obligation.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation directly contradicts the last sentence of section 5. Originally, the SUPPLIER remained responsible for employee compliance with the NDA, even after advising them of their obligations. This change relieves the SUPPLIER of that responsibility, as long as they have informed the employee of their obligations. This creates a significant conflict within the section itself, undermining the enforcement of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Any disclosure of Confidential Inform ation shall be limited SUPPLIER’s employees and who have a need to use or study such Confidential Information for the Purpose. SUPPLIER shall advise its employees and agen ts of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement. SUPPLIER will not be held responsible to ensure their compliance, as long as they have advised the employee of their obligation.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies section 5 to relieve the SUPPLIER of responsibility for ensuring employee compliance with the NDA, as long as the employee has been advised of their obligations. The contradicted text is the changed text, which now explicitly states that the SUPPLIER will not be held responsible. This creates a direct conflict within the section itself, undermining the enforcement of the agreement because the original text states that the supplier is responsible for compliance, and the changed text negates this responsibility.

perturbation.original_text: 6. All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of GK. At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK and shall certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of GK, unless GK fails to enforce this NDA within 1 year. At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK and shall certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless GK fails to enforce this NDA within 1 year' to the section stating that all Confidential Information remains the property of GK, this introduces a condition where ownership of the information could potentially transfer to the SUPPLIER if GK doesn't actively enforce the NDA, contradicting the initial statement that the information 'shall remain' GK's property. This new term affects section 7, where it mention that 'Confidential Information may only be used by SUPPLIER in connection with the pursuance of the Purpose', so the supplier can claim ownership due to GK failing to enforce the NDA within a year.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Confidential Information may only be used by SUPPLIER in connection with the pursuance of the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 6 introduces a condition where ownership of Confidential Information could transfer to the Supplier if GK fails to enforce the NDA within one year. This directly contradicts the original statement that all Confidential Information 'shall remain' the property of GK. This contradiction impacts Section 7, as the supplier can then potentially claim ownership of the information and challenge the restriction that 'Confidential Information may only be used by SUPPLIER in connection with the pursuance of the Purpose,' thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. SUPPLIER shall not assign or otherwise tran sfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third party without the prior written consent of GK, and any attempted assignment or transfer without su ch prior written consent shall be null and void.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. SUPPLIER shall not assign or otherwise tran sfer any of its rights or obligations under this Agreement to any third party without the prior written consent of GK, except to its subsidiaries, and any attempted assignment or transfer without su ch prior written consent shall be null and void.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'except to its subsidiaries' to the clause prohibiting assignment or transfer of rights and obligations introduces a contradiction. While the original clause states *any* assignment requires GK's prior written consent, the exception allows transfer to subsidiaries without such consent. This affects the 'entire agreement' clause in section 9, because by giving the change above, this creates the possibility of a transfer of confidential information with no written consent. This creates in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. This Agreement sets out and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the disclosure, protection and use of Confidential Information and supersedes all prior agreements, whether oral or written, relating to such matters and all other prior communications between the pa rties relating to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause 8 to allow assignment of rights and obligations to subsidiaries without GK's prior written consent, which directly contradicts clause 9 stating that the agreement constitutes the entire agreement regarding confidential information and supersedes all prior agreements. This creates a contradiction because confidential information could now be transferred to subsidiaries without written consent, violating the entire agreement clause. The explanation provided directly mentions this contradiction, and it aligns with the 'Structural Flaws - In Text Contradiction' definition where a clause's modification introduces conflicting implications within the document.
